nodes.context: Italian American Mafia crime family based in Cleveland, Ohio, involved in labor racketeering, casino skimming, loansharking, and illegal gambling.
Italian American gang formed in Cleveland's Little Italy around 1913, focused on bootlegging and later became the core of the Cleveland crime family.
Sicilian immigrant brothers who broke away from the Lonardos and formed their own faction, vying for control of the Cleveland underworld in the 1920s.
Sicilian immigrant brothers who initially led the Cleveland crime family before being killed by the Porrellos.
Network of powerful criminals including Lucky Luciano and Meyer Lansky, formed in 1931.
Mafia's governing body, of which Frank Milano was a charter member.
Chicago-based Mafia family; had ties with the Cleveland family under Scalish.
New York Mafia family; had ties with the Cleveland family under Scalish.
Pittsburgh-based Mafia family; had an arrangement with the Cleveland family regarding Youngstown rackets.
Kansas City-based Mafia family; partnered with Cleveland and Milwaukee families in Teamsters pension fund schemes.
Milwaukee-based Mafia family; partnered with Cleveland and Kansas City families in Teamsters pension fund schemes.
Irish gang led by Danny Greene that competed with the Cleveland family for rackets in the 1970s.
Italian American gang operating in Collinwood, Cleveland, in the 1910s.
Group of criminals engaged in armed robbery, auto theft, and burglary based at the Baldwin Water Treatment Plant reservoir.
One of the Five Families of New York City, with a faction in Rochester, New York.
Italian American Mafia organization based in Detroit, Michigan.
Italian American Mafia family based in New Jersey.
Outlaw motorcycle club, listed as an ally of the Cleveland family.
Irish gang in Cleveland, rival of the Cleveland crime family.

edges.context: The Mayfield Road Mob evolved into the Cleveland crime family; Frank Milano and Alfred Polizzi were leaders of both.
The Porrellos broke away from the Lonardos in 1926 and killed Joseph and John Lonardo in 1927.
Joseph Porrello became boss of the Cleveland family after killing the Lonardos, ruling from 1927 to 1930.
The Mayfield Road Mob, led by Frank Milano, fought and eventually killed the Porrello brothers, taking over the Cleveland family.
Frank Milano joined the National Crime Syndicate in 1931.
Frank Milano was a charter member of The Commission.
Under Scalish, the Cleveland family developed ties with the Chicago Outfit.
Under Scalish, the Cleveland family developed ties with the Genovese family.
The Cleveland family had an arrangement entitling them to 25% of Pittsburgh's Youngstown rackets.
The Cleveland, Kansas City, and Milwaukee families partnered to influence the Teamsters pension fund.
The Cleveland, Kansas City, and Milwaukee families partnered to influence the Teamsters pension fund.
A violent mob war erupted in the late 1970s between the Cleveland family and Danny Greene's gang, resulting in numerous car bombings.
Fought a mob war from 1978 to 1981 over control of Youngstown rackets.
Associate Anthony Delmonti worked with the Bonanno family's Rochester faction.
Iacobacci forged ties with the Detroit Partnership.
Iacobacci forged ties with the DeCavalcante crime family.
Listed as an ally in the infobox.
Listed as a rival in the infobox.
